section.title: Hannah's Prayer
section.sectionRationale: Hannah's answered prayer overflows into a song, and the song sounds the keynote of the whole book. She does not merely thank God for a baby; she praises the God who reverses the world, breaking the bows of the mighty and lifting the poor from the dust. The song rises to a startling climax about a king and an anointed one who do not yet exist, so that a private thanksgiving becomes a prophecy of the kingdom to come. It is one movement: the theology of the entire book, sung.
section.thesis: The God who opened one barren womb is the God who topples the proud and exalts the humble, who kills and makes alive, and who will judge the earth and exalt the horn of His anointed King, so that Hannah's small deliverance becomes a window onto God's great reversal and the coming of His Messiah.
section.frameNote: Three movements of the song: praise for the God of reversal, verses 1 to 3; the great reversals He works, verses 4 to 8; and the LORD who judges the earth and exalts His anointed King, verses 9 to 10, with Samuel left ministering, verse 11.

units.label: Praise for the God of reversal
units.note: there is no Rock like our God

verses.text: At that time Hannah prayed: “My heart rejoices in the LORD; my {{qeren|horn}} is exalted in the LORD. My mouth speaks boldly against my enemies, for I rejoice in Your salvation.
There is no one holy like the LORD. Indeed, there is no one besides You! And there is no {{tsur|Rock}} like our God.
Do not boast so proudly, or let arrogance come from your mouth, for the LORD is a God who knows, and by Him actions are weighed.
The bows of the mighty are broken, but the feeble are equipped with strength.
The well-fed hire themselves out for food, but the starving hunger no more. The barren woman gives birth to seven, but she who has many sons pines away.
The LORD brings death and gives life; He brings down to Sheol and raises up.
The LORD sends poverty and wealth; He humbles and He exalts.

He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap. He seats them among princes and bestows on them a throne of honor. For the foundations of the earth are the LORD’s, and upon them He has set the world.

units.label: The LORD judges the earth and exalts His anointed
units.note: He will exalt the horn of His anointed

verses.text: He guards the steps of His faithful ones, but the wicked perish in darkness; for by his own strength shall no man prevail.

Those who oppose the LORD will be shattered. He will thunder from heaven against them. The LORD will judge the ends of the earth and will give power to His king. He will exalt the horn of His {{mashiach|anointed}}.”
Then Elkanah went home to Ramah, but the boy began ministering to the LORD before Eli the priest.

qeren.gloss: Horn. Drawn from the horn of a strong animal, it is a vivid image of strength, dignity, and victory. Hannah says her horn is exalted, her strength lifted up by the LORD; the song ends with God exalting the horn of His anointed. The word frames the whole prayer, from one woman's rescue to the strength of the coming King.

tsur.gloss: Rock. A favorite Old Testament name for God, picturing Him as unshakable refuge and sure foundation, Deuteronomy 32. Against a world of shifting fortunes and toppling powers, Hannah confesses there is no Rock like our God, the one steady ground beneath every reversal she sings.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one, messiah. The one set apart and empowered by God, marked out by anointing oil. Here, before Israel has any king, Hannah prophesies that God will exalt the horn of His anointed. It is the first sounding of a note that will swell through David to the true Messiah, Jesus Christ, the Anointed King.

basic.context: Once God answers her prayer and gives her Samuel, Hannah breaks into a song. But she does not simply celebrate her baby; she sings about the kind of God who gave him. He is the God who turns the world upside down: He breaks the strong and strengthens the weak, empties the full and fills the hungry, brings low and lifts up. At the very end she sings about a king and an anointed one, even though Israel has no king yet, so her thanksgiving becomes a prophecy. The big question the song answers is: what is God like? He is the God who lifts the lowly and will one day exalt His Messiah.
basic.hermeneutics: This is poetry, a song of praise, and it uses vivid images to make its point, bows broken, the hungry filled, the barren bearing seven. We should read it as worship and as prophecy, not as a flat promise that every faithful person will grow rich or have many children. Its enduring truth is who God is, the God of the great reversal, and its climax looks beyond Hannah's day to the King God will anoint.
basic.originalLanguages: Hannah sings that her qeren, her horn, her strength, is exalted, and that there is no tsur, no Rock, like our God, the one steady foundation under a shifting world. The song rises to the mashiach, the anointed one, God's King, a word used here before any king exists. From one woman's rescue the song reaches to the coming Messiah.
basic.typology: Hannah's song is the pattern for Mary's Magnificat; both women sing of a God who scatters the proud and lifts the humble, on the eve of a God-given son. The line about God bringing down to the grave and raising up foreshadows the death and resurrection at the heart of the gospel. And the anointed King she sings of points past David to Christ, the true Anointed One.
basic.christ: The song ends not with Hannah but with the LORD's anointed, His mashiach, His King. Israel had no king when she sang, so this is prophecy: God will raise up an anointed one and exalt his horn. That hope runs through David to Jesus, the Christ, the true and final Anointed King, who was brought down to the grave and raised up, and who is exalted to judge the ends of the earth. Hannah's small reversal is a signpost to the great reversal accomplished in Him.
basic.god: The God of this song is holy, unique, and unrivaled, the Rock. He knows all things and weighs every action, so no proud boast escapes Him. He is utterly sovereign over life and death, wealth and poverty, honor and shame. And He uses that total sovereignty to a moral end: to humble the proud and exalt the lowly, and finally to establish His anointed King.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 1 gives Mary's Magnificat, modeled on this song. Psalm 113 sings the same reversal, raising the poor from the dust. Matthew 5 blesses the poor in spirit and the hungry. And Philippians 2 shows Christ humbled to death and then highly exalted, the great reversal in person.
basic.oneStory: Hannah's thanksgiving becomes the theme song of 1 Samuel and of Scripture itself: God brings down the proud and lifts up the lowly. Her closing words about the anointed King reach past David to Christ. What God did in one barren womb He is doing in the whole story, running toward the Messiah whose horn He exalts forever.

context.literary: A psalm of thanksgiving set at the seam between Samuel's birth and the story of Eli's house. Placed here deliberately, it interprets everything that follows: the fall of the proud house of Eli and the rise of the lowly Samuel, and eventually the fall of Saul and the rise of David. Its final word, anointed, forms an arch with the very end of the book of 2 Samuel, framing the whole work.
context.historical: Songs of thanksgiving accompanied answered prayer and deliverance in Israel's worship. The horn is a common ancient image of strength and dignity. The mention of a king and an anointed one is striking in a book that opens in the kingless chaos at the end of the judges; the song looks ahead to the monarchy God is about to establish.
context.canonical: This song stands with the Song of Moses, the Song of Deborah, and Mary's Magnificat as a great biblical hymn of God's saving reversals. Its climactic reference to the LORD's anointed introduces the messianic hope that dominates Samuel, Kings, and the Psalms, and that the New Testament sees fulfilled in Jesus the Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To interpret the whole book in advance, teaching that the LORD sovereignly humbles the proud and exalts the lowly, and to sound the first note of the messianic hope, the anointed King whose horn God will exalt.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The song describes and celebrates God's character and His pattern of reversal; it is not a prescription guaranteeing wealth or children to the faithful. What endures is the truth about God, the Rock who lifts the lowly and judges the proud, and the prophetic hope of His anointed King fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: How can Hannah sing of the LORD's king and anointed when Israel has no king yet?

views.label: Genuine prophecy given by the Spirit
views.case: Hannah, or the Spirit speaking through her placed song, looks ahead to the monarchy God is about to raise up; the song functions as prophecy, setting the messianic hope at the very door of the book before any king exists.

views.label: The narrator's inspired framing of the whole book
views.case: The song is positioned by the author to interpret all that follows, anticipating Saul and David and reaching toward the anointed King; its royal climax is a deliberate literary and theological signpost, not an anachronism.

debates.bearing: Either way the closing line lifts the reader's eyes beyond Hannah's baby to the King God will anoint, a hope that runs through David to Christ, the true Messiah.

debates.question: Is the promised reversal material and immediate, or deeper and ultimate?

views.label: Real reversals in history
views.case: God truly does raise the poor and humble the mighty within history, as He did for Hannah and will do in toppling Eli's house and Saul's throne; the song celebrates God's actual dealings, not mere metaphor.

views.label: A pattern that reaches its fullness in the gospel
views.case: The reversals point beyond any single instance to God's ultimate work in Christ, who was brought low and raised up, and who blesses the poor in spirit; the deepest fulfillment is eschatological and messianic.

debates.bearing: Both are true and held together: God acts within history and points beyond it, so that Hannah's rescue and Christ's exaltation are of one piece, the same God working the same great reversal.

study.originalLanguages: The song is stitched together with a few weighty words. Hannah's qeren, her horn, the ancient image of strength and dignity, is exalted by the LORD, and the song ends by exalting the qeren of His anointed, so the whole prayer arches from one woman's raised strength to the King's. She confesses there is no tsur, no Rock, like our God: against a world where bows break and fortunes turn, God alone is the unshakable foundation, the name Moses gave Him in his own song, Deuteronomy 32. And the climax lands on the mashiach, the anointed one, God's King, a word sung here before Israel has any king at all. From the rescue of a barren woman the song reaches all the way to the Messiah, the note that will swell through David and find its fullness in Jesus, the Anointed King whose horn God exalts forever.

typology.type: Hannah's song of the God who lifts the lowly
typology.fulfillment: Mary's Magnificat, sung on the eve of Christ's birth, echoing Hannah almost line by line, Luke 1:46-55.

typology.type: The LORD who brings down to the grave and raises up
typology.fulfillment: The death and resurrection of Christ, the great reversal at the heart of the gospel, Philippians 2:8-9.

typology.type: The anointed King whose horn God exalts
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the Messiah, the true and final Anointed One, exalted to judge the ends of the earth, Acts 2:36 and Revelation 11:15.

study.christ: Hannah's song does not close on her baby but on the LORD's anointed, His mashiach, His King. There was no king in Israel when she sang, so the last line is pure prophecy: God will raise up an anointed one and exalt his horn. That hope runs through David, to whom God promised an everlasting throne, and comes to rest in Jesus, the Christ, the true and final Anointed King. He is the One who was brought down to the grave and raised up, verse 6 in person, and who is exalted to judge the ends of the earth. Hannah's small, personal reversal, a closed womb opened, is a signpost pointing to the great reversal God accomplishes in His Messiah, in whom the proud are scattered and the lowly are lifted up forever.
study.god: The God Hannah sings is holy and utterly unique, the Rock beside whom there is no other. He is all-knowing, weighing every action, so that no arrogant boast slips past Him. He is sovereign over the deepest realities, life and death, poverty and wealth, honor and shame, holding the very foundations of the earth. And He wields that total sovereignty toward a moral and gracious end: to break the proud and lift the lowly, to guard His faithful ones, and at last to establish and exalt His anointed King. This is a God worth trusting in every reversal, because the reversals are His, and He turns them toward the humble.

crossReferences.note: Mary's Magnificat is modeled on Hannah's song, praising the God who scatters the proud and exalts the humble.
The LORD raises the poor from the dust and gives the barren woman a home, the very reversal Hannah sings.
Christ humbled to death and then highly exalted, the great reversal of verse 6 embodied in the gospel.
God has made this Jesus both Lord and Christ, the anointed King whose horn is exalted.

study.oneStory: Hannah's thanksgiving is the overture to the whole book and to Scripture: the LORD brings down the proud and lifts up the lowly. Her final word, the anointed King, reaches past the coming of Saul and David to Christ Himself. What God did in one barren womb He is doing across the whole story, humbling the mighty, exalting the humble, and moving history toward the Messiah whose horn He exalts forever.

quiz.q: What is Hannah's song mainly about?

options.text: The character of God, who humbles the proud and exalts the lowly, and who will exalt His anointed King.
options.note: The song moves from Hannah's rescue to the God of reversal and His coming Messiah.

options.text: Her thankfulness that God gave her a baby son.
options.note: That is the occasion, but the song's real subject is the God who gave him and the reversal He works.

options.text: A set of promises that faithful people will always become wealthy.
options.note: It celebrates God's reversals, not a guarantee of riches; that misreads the poetry.

options.text: A complaint against God for her years of barrenness.
options.note: The song is joyful praise, not complaint.

quiz.q: Why is it striking that Hannah sings about the LORD's king and anointed?

options.text: Israel had no king yet, so her song functions as prophecy pointing to the coming Messiah.
options.note: The royal climax sets the messianic hope at the door of the book before any king exists.

options.text: It anticipates the monarchy God is about to establish through Samuel.
options.note: True, but understates that the line reaches beyond Saul and David to the true Anointed One.

options.text: It shows Hannah wanted her own son Samuel to become king.
options.note: Samuel was a prophet and judge, not king; the anointed one points elsewhere.

options.text: It proves the song was written long after and has no prophetic weight.
options.note: The passage presents it as genuine, forward-looking praise, not a mere later insertion.

quiz.q: How does Hannah's song connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Mary's Magnificat echoes it, and its anointed King is fulfilled in Jesus the Christ.
options.note: Luke 1 models Mary's song on Hannah's, and the Messiah is the Anointed One she foresees.

options.text: It is echoed by another woman's song at the announcement of a God-given son.
options.note: True of Mary, but this leaves out the fulfillment of the anointed King in Christ.

options.text: It is quoted by Jesus to describe the end of the world.
options.note: There is no such quotation; the link is the reversal theme and the Messiah.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: The Magnificat and the messianic hope tie it closely to the gospel.

quiz.q: What does the song teach about God's sovereignty?

options.text: He rules over life and death, wealth and poverty, and uses that power to humble the proud and lift the lowly.
options.note: Verses 6 to 8 join God's total sovereignty to His moral purpose of reversal.

options.text: He is able to raise the poor and bring down the mighty.
options.note: True, but omits the sweep of His rule over life, death, and the earth's foundations.

options.text: He controls only the outcomes of battles between armies.
options.note: His sovereignty here reaches far wider than warfare, over life and death itself.

options.text: He leaves the fortunes of people entirely to chance.
options.note: The song insists the LORD Himself sends poverty and wealth, and humbles and exalts.

quiz.q: Why does Hannah call God the Rock?

options.text: Because in a world of toppling powers and shifting fortunes, He alone is the unshakable, holy foundation.
options.note: The Rock names God as steady refuge amid the reversals she sings, echoing Deuteronomy 32.

options.text: Because God is stronger than her enemies who taunted her.
options.note: True in part, but the title means far more than strength against foes; it means unshakable steadiness.

options.text: Because rocks were used to build the sanctuary at Shiloh.
options.note: The title is a picture of God's steadfast nature, not a reference to building materials.

options.text: Because God is cold and unfeeling toward His people.
options.note: The Rock speaks of steadfast refuge and care, not coldness.

questions: Hannah's answered prayer overflowed into worship that was all about God, not just His gift. When God answers you, do you praise the Giver more than the gift?
The song celebrates a God who lifts the lowly and humbles the proud. Where in your life do you need to stop trusting your own strength, by which no man prevails, and rest on the Rock?
Hannah rejoiced in God's salvation before she could see the whole story. How can her confidence in God's reversals steady you while you wait in a hard or unresolved season?
The song ends by looking ahead to God's anointed King. How does it change your worship to know that Jesus is that Messiah, exalted to judge the earth and to save His people?
Mary later sang almost the same song. What would it look like for your prayers of thanksgiving to be as full of God's character and purposes as Hannah's was?

translationNotes.note: Horn renders qeren, an image of strength and dignity; the BSB footnote allows strength, and the same word returns in verse 10 of the anointed one.
Rock, tsur, is a title for God as unshakable refuge, drawn from the Song of Moses in Deuteronomy 32.
Anointed renders mashiach, messiah; sung before Israel had any king, it points ahead through David to Jesus the Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 2:1-11. Verses 1 to 10 are Hebrew poetry, rendered here as running lines; poem and line-break markers in the source were ignored. No em or en dashes anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
